title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Apollo Moon landings. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.text: On July 20, 1969, an estimated 650 million people watched a grainy black-and-white television feed as Neil Armstrong descended the ladder of the Lunar Module. It was the climax of a decade-long effort that had pushed the limits of human technology and courage. The Apollo program was not just a scientific mission; it was a geopolitical statement of intent in the heat of the Cold War. ||(41)|| This ambitious goal was set by President John F. Kennedy in 1961, at a time when the United States was trailing behind the Soviet Union in the space race. To achieve it, NASA had to invent almost everything from scratch, from the massive Saturn V rockets to the complex onboard computers that were less powerful than a modern digital watch. ||(42)|| The most dangerous of these was the Apollo 1 fire in 1967, which claimed the lives of three astronauts during a pre-launch test. The tragedy nearly derailed the entire program, forcing a complete redesign of the command module and a total overhaul of NASA’s safety protocols. ||(43)|| However, the lessons learned from that disaster paved the way for the success of Apollo 8, the first manned mission to orbit the Moon. As the crew emerged from the 'dark side' of the lunar surface and saw the Earth rising over the horizon, the perspective of humanity changed forever. We were no longer a collection of nations, but a fragile blue marble in a vast, indifferent void. ||(44)|| The landing itself was a masterpiece of precision and nerves. With only thirty seconds of fuel remaining, Armstrong took manual control to avoid a boulder-strewn crater, eventually touching down on the Sea of Tranquility. The words 'The Eagle has landed' signaled the successful completion of the most complex logistical task in history. ||(45)|| Six more missions followed, exploring different regions of the lunar surface and bringing back hundreds of pounds of moon rocks. These samples have since revolutionized our understanding of the solar system’s formation, proving that the Moon was likely created from a massive collision between Earth and a Mars-sized planet billions of years ago. ||(46)|| As we look toward the future with the Artemis program, the legacy of Apollo serves as a blueprint for what is possible when a nation’s resources and will are focused on a single, clear objective. The Moon was never the final destination; it was the first step on a journey that will eventually lead us to the stars.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, the [41] pressure to 'win' the race [/41] was the primary driver of the massive funding required. The objective was clear: to land a man on the Moon and return him safely to the Earth before the decade was out, a feat that many experts at the time thought impossible.
paragraphs.B: Throughout [42] this period of rapid development [/42], there were several near-catastrophic setbacks that threatened to end the dream of a lunar landing. Engineering challenges were constant, and the risk of failure was present in every bolt and circuit of the spacecraft.
paragraphs.C: This [43] period of mourning and reflection [/43] led to a new culture of 'disciplined engineering' at NASA. Every component was scrutinized, and every potential failure point was addressed, ensuring that the subsequent missions would be the most reliable machines ever built.
paragraphs.D: This [44] iconic 'Earthrise' photograph [/44] became a symbol of the burgeoning environmental movement. It served as a powerful reminder of our planet's isolation and beauty, sparking a global conversation about the need to protect our only home.
paragraphs.E: Following this [45] historic milestone [/45], the focus of the program shifted from mere survival to scientific discovery. The later Apollo missions were equipped with lunar rovers, allowing astronauts to travel miles away from their landing site to conduct geological surveys.
paragraphs.F: Despite the [46] conclusion of the program in 1972 [/46], the data collected continues to be analyzed by scientists today. The technological spin-offs, from water purification systems to flame-retardant fabrics, have become integrated into our daily lives in ways we often take for granted.
paragraphs.G: The [47] Soviet Luna program [/47] was also making significant strides at this time. Although they never succeeded in sending a man to the Moon, their robotic landers were the first to return lunar soil samples to Earth, providing a different but equally valuable set of data.

questions.explanation: A refers to the 'pressure to win the race' mentioned in the context of the Cold War and defines the 'ambitious goal' mentioned in the following paragraph.
B introduces the 'near-catastrophic setbacks' which leads directly into the specific example of the 'Apollo 1 fire' described in the next paragraph.
C refers to the 'period of mourning' after the Apollo 1 fire and explains the redesign and safety overhaul mentioned before the gap.
D links the description of 'Earth rising over the horizon' to the 'Earthrise photograph' and its impact on the environmental movement.
E refers to the 'historic milestone' (the first landing) and explains how subsequent missions shifted their focus toward scientific exploration.
F marks the 'conclusion of the program' and links the historical events to the 'legacy' and 'modern applications' discussed in the final paragraph.
